title: Structural characterization of new poly(ethylene oxide)-based alkaline solid polymer electrolytes
abstract:
Structural Characterization of New Poly(ethylene oxide)-
Based Alkaline Solid Polymer Electrolytes
E. SALMON, 1 S. GUINOT, 1 M. GODET, 2 J. F. FAUVARQUE 1
1 CNAM, Electrochimie Industrielle, 2 rue Conte´, 75141 Paris Cedex 03, France
2 CNAM, Physico-chimie et Mise en Œuvre des Mate´riaux Macromole´culaires, 292 rue Saint-Martin,
75141 Paris Cedex 03, France
Received 27 June 1996; accepted 30 December 1996
ABSTRACT:
A new class of alkaline solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs) based on poly(eth-
ylene oxide) (PEO), potassium hydroxide (KOH), and water was investigated. The
structure of the SPEs was studied by differential scanning calorimetry, thermogravime-
tric analysis (TGA), X-ray diffraction, and optical microscopy techniques. The existence
of a crystalline complex between PEO, KOH, and H2O was evidenced for some composi-
tions, depending on the O/K ratio. A possible structure was proposed, and a schematic
phase diagram was established for this PEO–KOH–H2O system. The ﬁrst conductivity
measurements also revealed the great interest of these systems, with conductivity
values up to 10 03 S/cm. q 1997 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Appl Polym Sci 65: 601–607, 1997
Key words:
solid polymer electrolytes; poly(ethylene oxide); potassium hydroxide;
structure; crystalline complex

This is the expected behavior, and it suggests that
a minimum amount of KOH is necessary for the
crystallization of the complex to occur. Below this
minimum concentration, KOH is fully solvated by
the polymeric matrix and/or the water present
and no crystallization can be observed. Moreover,
the observation that a pure crystalline polymeric
phase A remains almost unchanged by the pres-
ence of the salt seems to suggest that the forma-
tion and crystallization of the complex surpris-
ingly occur with the contribution of the amor-

the same temperature in all compositions sug-
Tg is lower than that of pure PEO containing the
same amount of water (Fig. 6); it seems that
there is a combined plasticizing effect of KOH and
water. On the other hand, for the compositions
with a certain amount of crystalline complex (e.g.,
O/K Å 1.55), the melting temperature of which
is higher than that of pure PEO, Tg is found to be
greater than expected (Fig. 6). The crystallization
of the complex, which we showed occurs in the
amorphous phase of the SPE, seems to hinder
chain ﬂexibility, thus increasing the glass transi-
tion temperature; the related transition is then
strongly diminished. One of the studied composi-
tions showing high complex content (with a high
melting temperature) even exhibits a second Tg
at a temperature signiﬁcantly higher, suggesting
that a multiphase material is obtained. Generally,
most values of Tg range from approximately 083
to 0687C, whereas Tg of pure PEO is measured at
This behavior is signiﬁcantly different from the
reports in the literature, which usually describe
an increase of the glass transition temperature
of the polymeric matrix when an alkaline salt is
added. It seems that the main point in our sys-
tems is the presence of water, although its precise

sitions allowed us to reach some important conclu-

tallization (or fusion) of the water is observed on
gested a solid–solid transition. This could be a
reorganization of the complex or the transition
heating, which means that the water is certainly
bound, because of its dual role as a solvent of both
between two complexes of different well-deﬁned
stoichiometries, possibly involving water. The
ﬁrst form of the complex, which only appears at
sufﬁciently low temperature, seems to be favored
by slow cooling of the sample. The transition tem-
perature is very close to room temperature, so
that this form of the complex is stable only at
low temperatures. This is conﬁrmed by the ﬁrst-
heating DSC traces, which usually show no endo-
therm at 27–327C. These DSC results allowed us
to suggest a phase diagram for the PEO–KOH–
H2O compositions (see Fig. 5).
The experiments carried out at very low tem-
perature in order to determine the glass transi-
tion temperature Tg of the SPEs also indicate the
same trend. All values determined turned out to
be smaller than the Tg of pure PEO. In order to
allow accurate comparisons, Tg was also mea-
KOH. The results indicate that, in the SPEs with-
DSC traces showing glass transitions for
out crystalline complex phase (e.g., O/K Å 5.1),
ried out with the related PEO-water compositions
also exhibit a one-step process at temperatures
between approximately 425 and 4317C. These re-
sults are in agreement with other SPE systems
where the presence of an inorganic salt usually
decreases the degradation temperature of the ma-
trix, as a result of interactions between the poly-
mer backbone and the solvated ions.18
On the other hand, when compositions that
contain crystalline complex are studied (e.g., O/K
Å 2.55), a third step in the degradation mecha-
nism is observed with a maximum rate at about
5007C. No ﬁrm explanation has been given to this
phenomenon, which is still under investigation.
It is likely that, in both cases, the ﬁrst degrada-
tion step corresponds to the decomposition of the
PEO phase, while the other steps result from fur-
ther degradation of the decomposition products.
However, it is important to notice that a signiﬁ-
cant difference can be observed in the degradation
TGA curves of different systems.

The photographs taken during optical micros-
copy experiments also exhibit sharp differences
between the compositions. In some SPEs, namely
those which only show one endotherm (A) in DSC
experiments, only one crystalline phase appears
at approximately 607C and it is very similar to
the crystalline phase of PEO, i.e., it exhibits a
spherulitic-type crystallization (typical Maltese
cross). In the case of the SPEs where an endo-
therm corresponding to the polymer-salt complex
is present (C), two different phases can be ob-
served on cooling. The phase which crystallizes at
higher temperature (T É 1107C) is quite different
from the spherulitic type. Indeed, the photo-
graphs show a phase without radial orientation
(no Maltese cross). The second phase crystallizes
at a lower temperature (T É 607C) and is similar
to the spherulitic type of PEO. Such morphology
containing two crystalline phases was already ob-
served in the case of PEO-Li salt compositions.19
The investigation of this new family of PEO-based
alkaline electrolytes containing potassium hy-
droxide and water is only at its beginning, but the
ﬁrst conductivity measurements (which will be
reported elsewhere) revealed the great interest
of these materials for applications to secondary
batteries. Indeed, the conductivity at room tem-
perature can reach 10 03 S/cm for some composi-
tions. The study of the structure of these SPEs
was performed in order to establish the relation-
